coredata.dc:title: A novel coumarin-based chemosensor for colorimetric detection of Ag(I) ion and fluorogenic sensing of Ce(III) ion
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Mingyue
Xu, Zhenxiang
Song, Yanxi
Li, Hongqi
Xian, Chunying
coredata.dc:description: A novel coumarin dye was synthesized, which acts as a chemosensor for selective detection of Ag+ and Ce3+ ions in aqueous solution. Upon addition of Ag+ to the sensor solution a new absorption band centered 580 nm was observed and the color of the solution changed from pale yellow to brown while other metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+) did not cause distinct change. The coumarin dye can also be used as a fluorescence turn-on chemosensor for selective detection of Ce3+ over other metal ions including common trivalent metal ions Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ as well as lanthanide metal ions Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+ and La3+. Investigation on changes in the intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm with the equivalent ratio of Ce3+/sensor (0–1.1) reveals an approximate linear relationship, from which the concentration of Ce3+ ion may be estimated. The detection limit of Ce3+ ion by the sensor is estimated to be 2.07 × 10–7 M.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Coumarin
Sensor
Colorimetric
Silver(I)
Cerium(III)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General UV–vis and fluorescence spectra measurements
Synthesis and structural characterization of coumarin dyes
Colorimetric detection of Ag+ ion by coumarin-based sensor L1
Fluorescent detection of Ce3+ ion by sensor L1

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported in part by “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 11D10525 )”.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectra of L1 before and after addition of different metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+). Experimental conditions: 2 × 10–5 M L1, 0.1 M HEPES buffer in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v), pH = 7.16.

Color of solution of L1 before and after addition of different metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+ and K+). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

Fluorescence emission (excitation wavelength 247 nm) spectra of L1 before and after addition of different metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+). Experimental conditions: 2 × 10–5 M L1, 0.1 M HEPES buffer in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v), pH = 7.16.

Fluorescence emission (excitation wavelength 247 nm) spectra of L1 upon addition of different lanthanide metal ions (Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+, La3+ and Ce3+). Experimental conditions: 2 × 10–5 M L1, 0.1 M HEPES buffer in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v), pH = 7.16.

Fluorescence emission spectra of L1 upon addition of different equivalent of Ce3+ ion (0‒1.1 equivalent to L1). Experimental conditions: 2 × 10–5 M L1, 0.1 M HEPES buffer in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v), pH = 7.16.

Relationship between the intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm and the ratio of Ce3+/L1 (0‒1.1). Experimental conditions: 2 × 10–5 M L1, 0.1 M HEPES buffer in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v), pH = 7.16.
Schematic representation of Ce3+-induced fluorescence enhancement.

head.ce:title: A novel coumarin-based chemosensor for colorimetric detection of Ag(I) ion and fluorogenic sensing of Ce(III) ion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering & Biotechnology, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering & Biotechnology, Donghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Penglai Xinguang Pigment Chemical Co., Ltd, Penglai 265601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Penglai Xinguang Pigment Chemical Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel coumarin dye was synthesized, which acts as a chemosensor for selective detection of Ag+ and Ce3+ ions in aqueous solution. Upon addition of Ag+ to the sensor solution a new absorption band centered 580 nm was observed and the color of the solution changed from pale yellow to brown while other metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+) did not cause distinct change. The coumarin dye can also be used as a fluorescence turn-on chemosensor for selective detection of Ce3+ over other metal ions including common trivalent metal ions Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ as well as lanthanide metal ions Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+ and La3+. Investigation on changes in the intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm with the equivalent ratio of Ce3+/sensor (0–1.1) reveals an approximate linear relationship, from which the concentration of Ce3+ ion may be estimated. The detection limit of Ce3+ ion by the sensor is estimated to be 2.07 × 10–7 M.

ce:section.ce:para: It is known that silver has been extensively used in electrical industry, photographic and imaging industry, and pharmacy. However, accumulation of Ag+ ions affects multiple targets in living systems. Therefore convenient and selective sensing of Ag+ ion is of high significance due to its potential toxicity but wide utility. Potentiometric probe [1], electrochemical biosensor [2,3] and interferometry [4] have been developed for detection of Ag+ ion. But these methods rely mainly on sophisticated instruments and experienced operators. Colorimetric [5,6] and fluorescent chemosensors [7–20] are better choices for rapid and selective detection of Ag+ ion. Some fluorescent probes can be utilized for simultaneous detection of Ag+ ion and other ions such as Hg2+ ion [21–23], Cu2+ ion [24], protons [25], Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions [26], Ni2+ and Pd2+ ions [27], and CN‒ ion [28]. On the other hand, as the most abundant element of the lanthanide group, cerium has wide industrial applications in the fields like lighting and television, metallurgy, glass and ceramics, and catalytic converters in vehicles. Owing to the enormous usage of cerium in various aspects of human life, the development of rapid and sensitive methods for cerium determination is of great significance. The present Ce3+ detection means mainly involve different instrumental methods, including X-ray fluorescence [29], inductively coupled plasma (ICP) [30–32], spectrophotometry [33], spectrofluorometry [34–36], and potentiometric sensors based on ion-selective electrodes [37–43]. Though these methods may provide sensitive and reproductive results, they suffer the requirement of bulky instrumentation. More recently colorimetric and fluorescent sensors for detection of Ce3+ received increasing attention for convenience and high selectivity and sensitivity [44–46]. By using DNAzyme hydrogel-based colorimetric method, Ln3+ can be detected with a limit of detection (LOD) of 20 nM for Ce3+ [44]. Luminol acts as fluorescent probe for lanthanide(III) ions [47] and tetramethylcucurbit [6] uril-triggered fluorescence emission has been utilized for recognition of La3+ and Ce3+ [48]. Grabchev and colleagues found that 1,8-naphthalimide-based fluorescent sensors were capable of detecting simultaneously Ag+ and Ce3+ [49,50]. However, Ag+ and Ce3+ could not be differentiated from each other and meanwhile the presence of Zn2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Co2+ and protons interfered with the selective detection. To our best knowledge development of bimodal fluorescent sensors for simultaneous detection and discrimination of Ag+ and Ce3+ is still a blank. In continuation of our study on multifunctional sensors [51,52], we have designed and synthesized a novel coumarin dye bearing propargyl and phenylhydrazone moieties, which acts as a highly selective and sensitive chemosensor for Ag+ and Ce3+. Herein we report the synthesis, structural characterization and recognition behavior of the new coumarin-based colorimetric and fluorescent sensor.

All materials and solvents employed in this study were of analytical grade. All chemical reagents were purchased from commercial sources and used as received unless other statements. 7-Hydroxy-3-acetylcoumarin was synthesized according to literature method [53]. Melting points were determined on a WRS-2A capillary melting apparatus and the quoted temperatures were uncorrected. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AM 400 spectrometer. DMSO-d 6 or CDCl3 was used as solvent and chemical shifts recorded were internally referenced to Me4Si (0 ppm). IR spectra were obtained on a Thermo Electron Corporation Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Mass spectra were obtained on an Agilent 6000 LC-MS instrument (ESI, pos mode, 70–1000 amu). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorometer. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a JASCO V530 spectrometer.

A mixture of 7-hydroxy-3-acetylcoumarin (1.00 g, 5 mmol), phenylhydrazine (0.54 g, 5 mmol) and tosic acid (5.00 mg, 0.025 mmol) in ethanol (30 mL) was heated to 80 °C and refluxed for 1 h. The reaction was monitored by thin layer chromatography and during the reaction process orange precipitation was formed. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and filtered. The solid was washed with ethanol for several times to give product 1a (1.01 g) as deep red powder. Yield 68%. Mp 215–217 °C. IR (KBr pellet): ν 3328, 3229, 1678, 1596, 1493, 1232, 1215, 1153, 1025, 739, 694 cm–1. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d 6): δ 10.64 (s, 1H), 9.33 (s, 1H), 8.10 (s, 1H), 7.67 (d, J = 8.5 Hz, 1H), 7.23–7.20 (m, 4H), 6.83–6.74 (m, 3H), 2.20 (s, 3H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO-d 6): δ 161.15 (C˭O), 159.96 (C–OH), 155.03 (C˭N), 145.70, 139.63 (C–NH), 139.26, 130.18, 128.82, 122.83, 119.08, 113.17, 112.93, 111.71, 101.68, 15.20 (CH3). MS (ESI): m/z 295.2 (M+ + 1).

To a flask was filled with 20 mL of anhydrous acetone under magnetic stirring. Then the flask was charged with compound 1a ( 0.294 g, 10 mmol) and potassium carbonate (0.138 g, 10 mmol). The mixture was stirred at 56 °C for 30 min. Propargyl bromide (0.119 g, 10 mmol) was added dropwise and the reaction mixture was refluxed for 5 h. After reaction the mixture was poured into 20 mL of water and extracted with ether (30 mL × 3). The combined organic extracts were washed with saturated brine and dried over MgSO4. The solvent was distilled off under reduced pressure to obtain the crude product which was purified by column chromatography eluting with petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (5:1, v/v) to afford L1 as lemon yellow solid (1.41 g, 85%). Mp 124–125 °C. IR (KBr pellet): ν 3352, 3300, 3035, 2114, 1692, 1599, 1503, 1239, 1211, 1132, 1013, 754, 698 cm–1. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.03 (s, 1H), 7.63–7.48 (m, 2H), 7.32–7.20 (m, 2H), 7.17–7.13 (m, 2H), 6.97–6.89 (m, 3H), 4.79 (d, J = 9.5 Hz, 2H, OCH2), 2.59 (s, 1H, C≡CH), 2.31 (s, 3H, CH3). 13C NMR (101 MHz, CDCl3): δ 160.66 (C˭O), 160.45, 155.33 (C˭N), 144.60, 139.81 (C–NH), 129.87, 129.41, 129.31, 126.88, 120.71, 113.89, 113.39, 113.34, 101.68, 77.39 (C≡C), 77.20 (C≡C), 56.28 (OCH2), 13.89 (CH3). MS (ESI): m/z 333.2 (M+ + 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: General UV–vis and fluorescence spectra measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The spectral analyses were accomplished at room temperature in 0.1 M HEPES buffer solution (EtOH/H2O = 9:1, v/v, pH = 7.16). The concentration of the sensor L1 for UV–vis and fluorescence measurement was 2 × 10–5 M. Solutions of metal ions were prepared with nitrate or chloride salts in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v). Fluorescence titration experiments were performed directly in 2 mL cuvette by successive addition of corresponding chemical reagent using a microliter syringe at an excitation wavelength of 411 nm. The slit for both excitation and emission was 5.0 nm. Upon addition of every aliquot, the solution was well mixed then the spectrum was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of coumarin dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The novel coumarin-based sensor L1 was synthesized by a two-step protocol from 7-hydroxy-3-acetylcoumarin, as showed in Scheme 1. Condensation between acetylcoumarin and phenylhydrazine catalyzed by tosic acid (5 mol%) in ethanol produced compound 1a in 68% yield. This hydrazone intermediate appeared in an article but no any physical and chemical data of the compound was reported therein [54]. Propargylation of the hydroxy group in 1a with propargyl bromide in the presence of potassium carbonate afforded sensor L1 in 85% yield. The chemical structures of 1a and L1 were fully characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, IR and mass spectrum. All of the data in the spectra were in good accordance with the structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Ag+ ion by coumarin-based sensor L1
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra of sensor L1 before and after addition of equal equivalent of different metal ions including Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+ were measured and showed in Fig. 1. The concentration of the sensor L1 and each salt CuCl2, HgCl2, AgNO3, ZnCl2, Ce(NO3)3, LiCl, NaCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, KCl and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O was 2 × 10–5 M. The sensor L1 exhibited a weak absorption band at around 360 nm. After addition of Ag+ ion the absorption band at 360 nm was enhanced and a new absorption band centered 580 nm appeared. Addition of other metal ions did not cause distinct change in the UV–vis spectrum of L1. Meanwhile the color change of L1 in EtOH/H2O (9:1, v/v) upon addition of different metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+ and K+) were investigated as showed in Fig. 2. The color of L1 solution changed from pale yellow to brown after addition of Ag+ ion while addition of other metal ions failed to cause obvious color change. These results indicated that L1 acted as an effective and selective colorimetric sensor for Ag+ ion in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Ce3+ ion by sensor L1
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence emission (excitation wavelength 247 nm) spectra of L1 before and after addition of different metal ions including Cu2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ce3+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+ were determined and showed in Fig. 3. The concentration of the sensor L1 and each salt CuCl2, HgCl2, AgNO3, ZnCl2, Ce(NO3)3, LiCl, NaCl, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, KCl and Cr(NO3)3·9H2O was 2 × 10–5 M. A very weak fluorescence emission band was visible between 330 and 420 nm in the spectrum of sensor L1. After addition of Ce3+ ion the fluorescence emission band was enhanced drastically. Addition of other metal ions did not bring about distinct change in the spectra of L1. Therefore L1 can be used as a fluorescence turn-on chemosensor for selective detection of Ce3+ over other metal ions (Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+) including common trivalent metal ions Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+.

It is known that the members of the lanthanide series have similar ionic radii and outer electronic structures, which result in their similar physical and chemical properties and difficulty to distinguish from each other. This can be evidenced by the recently reported chemosensors for lanthanide metal ions based on DNAzyme hydrogel [44] or luminol [47], which were unable to distinguish Ln3+ ions effectively. To investigate the selectivity of sensor L1 for Ce3+ over other common lanthanide metal ions, fluorescence emission (excitation wavelength 247 nm) spectra of L1 upon addition of several representative lanthanide metal ions (Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+, La3+ and Ce3+) were measured and showed in Fig. 4. The concentration of the sensor L1 and each salt Pr(NO3)3·6H2O, Yb(NO3)3·5H2O, Nd(NO3)3·6H2O, La(NO3)3·6H2O and Ce(NO3)3 was 2 × 10–5 M. It can be found that addition of Ce3+ ion induced drastic enhancement in the fluorescence emission at around 350 nm while addition of other Ln3+ ions did not cause distinct change in the fluorescence emission at 350 nm. The result demonstrates that sensor L1 is highly selective for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ce3+ over other Ln3+ metal ions (Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+ and La3+).

Influence of the amount of Ce3+ (0–1.1 equivalent to L1) on the fluorescence emission of L1 was investigated. Fluorescence emission spectra of L1 upon addition of different equivalent of Ce3+ ion (0‒1.1 equivalent to L1) were showed in Fig. 5. With the increase in the ratio of Ce3+/L1, the intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm increased gradually. If a plot was drawn based on the relationship between the intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm and the ratio of Ce3+/L1 (0–1.1), a line was obtained as showed in Fig. 6. The linear fitting equation can be depicted as Y = 37384.55421 + 2.70 × with R = 0.98819, from which the detection limit is calculated to be 2.07 × 10–7 M. The sensor L1 exhibited good repeatability and may be used for quantitative determination of Ce3+ ion.

Possible mechanism of the fluorescence enhancement of the sensor L1 by Ce3+ ion is proposed and showed in Scheme 2. Sensor L1 bears a chelator site and readily complexes with Ce3+ ion. The complexation blocks the electron transfer from the nitrogen lone pair to the coumarin fluorophore. Thus the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process is suppressed by the complexation and the fluorescence is enhanced [55].

A novel coumarin dye L1 was designed and synthesized by a two-step protocol from 7-hydroxy-3-acetylcoumarin. The dye L1 was found to be able to recognize Ag+ and Ce3+ selectively in aqueous solution by colorimetric and fluorescence turn-on mode, respectively. Upon addition of Ag+ to L1 solution a new absorption band centered 580 nm was visible and the color of the solution changed from pale yellow to brown while other metal ions including Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Li+, Na+, Al3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, K+ and Cr3+ did not bring about distinct change in the spectra and color. The coumarin dye can also be used as a fluorescence turn-on chemosensor for selective detection of Ce3+ over other metal ions including common trivalent metal ions Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+ as well as lanthanide metal ions Pr3+, Yb3+, Nd3+ and La3+. Intensity of the fluorescence emission band at around 350 nm changed linearly with the equivalent ratio of Ce3+/sensor (0–1.1) and based on the relationship the concentration of Ce3+ ion may be determined. The detection limit of Ce3+ ion by L1 is estimated to be 2.07 × 10–7 M.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (11D10525)”.
